entry.title: Failed to resolve endpoint Error: Resolving Adapter Version Mismatch Issues in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
While working with SAP Cloud Integration - Integration Suite, consultants and developers often upgrade adapters to leverage new features, bug fixes, or security enhancements. However, upgrading adapters without understanding the design-time vs runtime behaviour can lead to confusing runtime errors.

In this blog, I’ll walk you through a very common issue that occurs when the adapter version is inconsistent between design time and runtime, why it happens, and how to fix it step by step. We will use SMB adapter as an example however, note that the solution described here is not limited to SMB adapter and applies to any adapter which is consumed from SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

Problem Statement : 

Let’s consider the following scenario where our SAP CI tenant has an iflow using SMB Adapter version 1.2.0 deployed at runtime. Let us assume SAP has announced a new version that has been released (1.3.0).

  1.  Navigate to Discover tab of your tenant and click Copy.
  2. Next, deploy the iflow.

When you execute the iFlow, it fails with an error like the one below:

[CONTENT][CONTENT_DEPLOY][InstanceError] : {"message":"EXCEPTION","childMessageInstances":[{"message":"CAUSE","childMessageInstances":[{"message":"CAUSE","parameters":["org.apache.camel.ResolveEndpointFailedException: Failed to resolve endpoint

At first glance, the configuration may look correct, credentials are valid, and yet we face the error as failed to resolve endpoint.

Why This Issue Occurs

One of the common root cause of this issue is adapter version mismatch between design time and runtime.

Here’s what happens internally:

  1. When we copy an adapter from Business Accelerator Hub/Discover, it is imported into design time only.
  2. The adapter is not deployed to runtime.
  3. Our iFlow is designed using the latest SMB Adapter 1.3.0 (design time).
  4. At runtime, it still tries to execute the iFlow using SMB Adapter 1.2.0 (runtime).

Since the runtime does not recognize parameters or capabilities introduced in the newer adapter version, it fails while resolving the endpoint.

In short, Design-time adapter version != Runtime adapter version = Execution failure

How to Fix the Issue (Step-by-Step)

Follow the steps below to resolve the SMB adapter version mismatch:

  1. Go to Discover > Integrations
  2. Search for SMB Adapter for SAP Integration Suite
  3. Click Copy to copy the latest version (1.3.0) into your tenant.

At this point, the adapter exists only in design time but you must also deploy the adapter to runtime as well.

This is the most important step and is often missed.

  1. Navigate to Design > Integrations and APIs
  2. Locate the newly copied SMB Adapter (1.3.0)
  3. Deploy the adapter. Click on Artifacts tab and then Click on three dots under Actions > Deploy

    Wait until the deployment status shows successful.

Now, the adapter version will be available at runtime as well. Redeploy your iflow. The iflow will now execute successfully without the endpoint resolution error.

Conclusion

Adapter upgrades in SAP Integration Suite are not just a design-time activity. Adapter versions must always be consistent between design time and runtime.

Whenever you upgrade an adapter from Discover or API Business Hub, always deploy it to runtime immediately before testing your iFlows.

This simple check can save hours of troubleshooting and prevent avoidable production issues.

Hope this blog helps fellow SAP CI consultants and developers facing similar adapter upgrade issues.

entry.title: Re: EBRR postings did not post
entry.content:
Good day @marsebjam 

Thank you for your question.

If FI Jan 2026 period was closed when the EBRR accounting entries were due, the system would not have created those FI posting in that closed period.

After reopening Jan, the standard approach is to rerun EBRR period end recalculation (you can use Run Revenue Recognition – Projects) so the missing WIP and unbilled revenue entries are posted. Consult  Revenue Recognition – Projects and Event-Based Revenue Recognition for more details.

I suggest you:

  1. Reopen FI period Jan 2026
  2. Trigger Revenue Recognition – Projects for Jan 2026 (same WBS scope).
  3. Check the application log and accounting documents.
  4. If need be, schedule the same app as a periodic background run to avoid gaps.

Should your posting still not appear after that, verify the project has a valid EBRR setup and recognition method. Recheck whether posting was already done in a later open period and reconcile the Event-Based Revenue Recognition analysis app and evaluate the document flow.

To summarise on your questions:

  • Will reopening Jan allow triggering the posting?
    • Yes, usually if you rerun Run Revenue Recognition – Projects for Jan.
  • Alternative automatic method?
    • I suggest you use scheduled periodic runs of Run Revenue Recognition – Projects. If a month is missed, execute an ad hoc catch-up run for that period in affected.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Implementing SAP BTP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI - enabling SSO and MFA with corporate IdP
entry.content:
Hi @tushar_bajaj77 ,

Thank you for the mention.

Please check if you have implemented the CCL related profile parameters correctly or not.

Profile Parameter

Value

Remarks

SETENV_XX

CCL_PROFILE=$(DIR_PROFILE)/DEFAULT.PFL

(XX is any free number from 00 to 99 which is not used elsewhere)

ccl/snc/namealias/value_1

, L=netherlands.accounts.ondemand.com, OU=secure-login-service, OU=Clients, O=SAP, C=DE

SNC name generated by SAP BTP SLS

ccl/snc/namealias/replacement_1

 

Keep it blank/empty, so that only CN=username is in use and Kerberos token can also be leveraged

ccl/snc/partner_case_x509

Upper

 

ccl/snc/server_partner_name_x509

EMailPrincipalOnlyOrSubject

 

ccl/snc/server_partner_name_mapping_for_SLC_only

1

 

Regards,

Aniket

entry.title: Re: overall release of purchase requisition vs Release of...
entry.content:
Dear @R971,

In Public Cloud you can’t use both approval modes overall/header + item-wise for the same PR document type

The PR release level is controlled by the PR document type in CBC Define Document Types for Purchase Requisitions (103385). When you set Overall Release for PR the document type is treated as header-level release only meaning it cannot be released item-by-item

SAP own guidance for Public Cloud flexible workflow setup is essentially:

  • Overall (header) release = tick both ovRelPReq and Scenario based workflow
  • Item level release = tick only Scenario based workflow and do not tick ovRelPReq

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3458210

So if you tick both overall and try to run item-wise workflows the system will behave exactly as you observed overall wins and item-level workflow won’t trigger

My Proposal and recommended:

Use one doc type for each approval mode for example:

  • Doc Type A (NB) -> Item level release (Scenario based workflow only)
  • Doc Type B (NBS or your custom type) ->Overall release (ovRelPReq + Scenario based workflow)

Then in Manage Workflows for Purchase Requisitions:

  • Maintain workflows for the Overall Release scenario restricted to the overall doc type + Company Code 1
  • Maintain workflows for the Release of Purchase Requisition Item scenario restricted to the item doc type + Company Code 2

This is the clean supported way because the scenario availability is driven by the document type setup

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: How to monitor IPS jobs using Cloud ALM
entry.content:
IPS service is available in Landscape management. How to configure Cloud ALM to be able to view status of all IPS jobs?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to monitor IPS jobs using Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Dear Customer, 

As described in SAP Expert Portal (and here) you need to:

  1. Have a subscription for SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Provisioning
  2. Have subscribed to SAP Cloud ALM (free of charge)
  3. Switch on data collection for your Cloud Identity Provisioning Service tenant in Job & Automation Monitoring

Steps with screenshots are provided in the document: https://support.sap.com/content/dam/support/en_us/library/ssp/alm/sap-cloud-alm/jobmonitoring/crun-job-and-auto-mon-cloud-ips-job.pdf

Best Regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Manage Fixed Assets- New Version
entry.content: Hi Yuki, From what I’ve seen, the “Manage Fixed Assets – New Version” app is typically released in future quarterly updates, not always immediately in the Q-system. Even if the business catalog is assigned, the app itself may not be available until the update is pushed to your tenant. Best practice is to monitor the SAP Release Notes for your S/4HANA Cloud version and plan user communications accordingly. That way you can avoid surprises when the app becomes available.

entry.title: Can a SAP Build Action be setup to stream output to a SAP Joule Skill?
entry.content:
We have an API which streams the AI Reasoning output in real-time.

We can surface the streamed output at a command line by calling our API as follows:

 try:
        with requests.post(
            API_URL,
            json=payload,
            headers=headers,
            stream=True,  # 👈 enables streaming
        ) as response:
        
Ideally, we'd like to present this streamed output in a SAP Joule Skill, with an action for our API. However,  the output is not streamed,  it returns only the final step of the output in one go, so not only is the full response not shown, it also takes longer to appear, which is not a good user experience.

Can Joule Skills/SAP Build Actions handle streamed output and how that acheived?

entry.title: Adapt UI changes mistakenly done in Test client (210) instead of Dev (200) – how to properly revert
entry.content:
We follow a standard landscape approach where client 200 is used for development/configuration and client 210 is used only for unit testing.

Originally, all development and configuration were done in 200, and the changes were moved to 210 for testing. After testing, a new requirement came to hide/edit some fields in a Fiori app.

While searching, I found the Key User role (SAP_UI_FLEX_KEY_USER) for Adapt UI, assigned it to a test user, and performed Adapt UI changes directly in client 210. I also activated those changes in 210.

Later, I realized this was incorrect, as Adapt UI changes should be created only in the development client (200) and then moved to 210 for testing.

To revert the mistake, I:

  • Opened Adapt UI in 210
  • Switched back to Original App and activated it

However:

  • There is no option to delete or discard Adapt UI versions
  • Older versions still exist (inactive)
  • I understand SAP does not support deleting UI Adaptation versions, only activating another version

My questions are:

  1. Is activating the Original App the correct and only supported way to revert mistaken Adapt UI changes done in 210?
  2. When I now create Adapt UI changes correctly in 200 and move them to 210 for testing, how can I ensure there is no conflict with the earlier versions created in 210?
  3. Are inactive Adapt UI versions (created earlier in 210) completely ignored once a new version from 200 is activated/published?
  4. What is the recommended best practice to avoid such conflicts when working across multiple clients (Dev → Test → Quality)?

Any guidance or SAP‑recommended approach would be appreciated.

entry.title: 🇧🇷 Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Belo Horizonte, Brazil)
entry.content:
This SAP CodeJam event is on Getting Started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core and Python. You do not need any background in the topic, but you should have a lot of curiosity!

In this CodeJam, you will learn how to use the Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core to implement retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). This approach improves the responses of large language models (LLMs) and reduces hallucinations. You will learn how to deploy an LLM and the orchestration service on SAP AI Core, and how to query it via SAP AI Launchpad and the Python SDK. Additionally, you will gain insights into key generative AI concepts and learn how to enhance RAG responses.

This is a free in-person event and is planned for Friday, March 20, 2026 in Belo Horizonte from 09:00 to 15:00. The language of the event is in Portuguese and the content is in English.

The event is part of the SIT Belo Horizonte that will take place the following day.

The instructors are @celofiorito and @EvertonMazzer !

Location: Localiza Labs, R. Gurupá, 33 - Cachoeirinha, Belo Horizonte - MG, 31150-180, Brasil

Please note that spaces are limited; we can only accommodate 50 attendees. If you wish to attend, register by clicking the Yes button in the Will you be attending? section above to the right. A few weeks before the event, we will ask you to confirm your attendance.

Prerequisites: Any Chromium-based web browser, such as Microsoft Edge, Google Chrome etc.

Visit the SAP CodeJam group for more information on SAP CodeJam events, to ask questions, and to chat with your fellow attendees.

entry.title: Re: CDS view for accessing customer group(sales area) in...
entry.content:
Good day @swarnali_basu39977a 

Thank you for your question.

I suggest you use I_CUSTOMERSALESAREA as the primary CDS view. For BAdI SD_SLS_CHECK_BEFORE_SAVE, you can read by Customer, Division, SalesOrganisation and DistributionChannel. Then get the customer group field from the sales area record.

See the following references for more details.

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3142371

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/784b14a35438498797ff8a6920079644.html

Should your tenant not expose the exact field name you need, I suggest you create a custom CDS view based on I_COSTOMERSALEAREA and consume that logic in your logic. Also, do not query only by customer.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Custom field visible in Order Confirmation PDF (In-Pr...
entry.content:
Dear @Ragunath_Selvam74,

Yes it’s possible but only if your custom field is available in the CDS-based data retrieval that SAP uses when the output item is Completed. What you’re seeing is a standard design difference in Pubic Cloud output control for sales documents:

  • In Preparation the PDF preview is rendered from transient (runtime) data of the sales order currently opened in the Sales Order apps
  • Completed the PDF is rendered by reading the data again from the database via a CDS view behind the form’s data source (FDP service). If the field isn’t available there it comes out blank

SAP Notes below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3632762

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3440654

What to do:

1. Verify you did NOT accidentally mismatch the extended data source vs. the form data source

Practical check:

  • Open the standard Order Confirmation form template you copied from and confirm its exact data source
  • Ensure your custom template uses that same data source not a different/renamed one

2. Ensure the field is available in the CDS-based rendering path

In practice that means the field must be part of the form data provider (FDP) data source / its underlying CDS view used at completion time. If your field only exists in the opened document runtime structures, it can show in In Preparation but disappear in Completed

3. SAP supported workaround if the needed data source can’t be extended

SAP supported workaround like in SAP Note above is:

  • Create a common custom field
  • Use custom logic to fill it
  • Add that common field to the form template

This is SAP recommended path when the exact required data source isn’t extensible for your original field

Summary:

SAP documents your exact symptom in SAP Note 3632762 (link above)

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: How to use custom font icons (TTF) in SAP MDK Avatar/...
entry.content: Yes, MDK supports using custom icon fonts.  To use it you will need to create a branded client.  The font icon files go in the /fonts directory in the .mdkproject structure.   They do not go in the MDK metadata.  Then you can reference them like you indicated in the metadata.

entry.title: Enhancing security: Enabling Multi-Factor Authentication enforcement for S-users
entry.content:
Starting from January 15, 2026, super administrators can enforce Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for their S-users. This new feature has been developed based on direct customer feedback and in response to the evolving security landscape, resulting in stronger protection for your user accounts.

What is Multifactor Authentication?

Multi-factor authentication, commonly known as MFA, is a powerful security measure that helps safeguard your accounts by requiring more than just a password. Instead of relying solely on something you know (like a password, PIN, or signature), MFA asks for an extra layer of verification, which could be:

  • Something you have: A one-time code generated by an authenticator app on your smartphone
  • Something you are: Biometrics, a fingerprint or a facial scan

By combining these different authentication factors, MFA makes it significantly tougher for attackers to break into your account. This is in fact one of the most effective ways to prevent unauthorized access and stop most data breaches.

Strengthening security with enhanced MFA Options for S-Users

Protecting critical SAP assets is crucial for our customers. Therefore, our approach to multi-factor authentication is evolving to meet this challenge. Now, super administrators can take a proactive role by enforcing MFA for S-users, while individuals still have the freedom to secure their accounts independently. This dual approach – administrator-led enforcement alongside voluntary enablement – offers the flexibility and meets modern security demands.

In the past, enabling MFA was left up to each S-user’s discretion. However, relying solely on voluntary enrollment is no longer sufficient to safeguard sensitive business information. By empowering both administrators and users, we’re making it easier to prevent unauthorized access and strengthen your organization’s security.

NEW scenario: Selective MFA enforcement by customer’s own super administrators

Now, super administrators can take a proactive role by enforcing MFA for S-users of their own company, while individuals still have the freedom to secure their accounts independently. Of course, this should be in line and aligned with the companies' own security policy.

Through the User Management Tool (UMT) in SAP for Me, super administrators have the option to activate MFA for S-users. This new feature allows administrators to: 

  • Enforce MFA: Search for, filter, and select specific S-users or all of them to make MFA mandatory for their logins.
  • Exclude technical users: Crucially, super administrators can exclude specific technical accounts (like those used for the BTP cloud connector) from the MFA requirement, ensuring that core business processes continue to run smoothly.

After MFA is enforced, the selected S-user(s) will receive an email notification with simple instructions on next steps and be guided through a one-time setup on their next login, ensuring a seamless and secure transition.

EXISTING scenario: Voluntary MFA enablement by the S-users themselves

The option for individual users to proactively secure their own accounts remains fully available. 

Any S-user can visit their profile page via SAP's profile management at any time to enable MFA for themselves. This has been a great option for security-conscious users who want to protect their accounts even before an administrator-led rollout.

Please note: MFA enforced by the super administrator overrides any voluntary setting previously configured by the user.

entry.title: Build a Winning IT Operating Model for Your S/4HANA Investment
entry.content:
Werde zum Value Partner: Entfessele das volle Potenzial der S/4HANA-Investition mit dem richtigen IT Operating Model!

Die digitale Transformation ist immer noch für viele Unternehmen ein zentrales strategisches Ziel – und SAP S/4HANA spielt dabei in vielen Unternehmen eine Schlüsselrolle. Ein maßgeschneidertes IT Operating Model (ITOM) ist die Voraussetzung dafür, dass die Potenziale von S/4HANA voll ausgeschöpft werden können. Dieser Beitrag zeigt, worauf es beim Aufbau eines ITOM im SAP-Kontext ankommt – und welche strategischen Entscheidungen dabei zu treffen sind.​ 

Ausgangslage 

Ein wirksames ITOM entsteht nicht im luftleeren Raum. Vielmehr hängt es entscheidend vom Reifegrad der bestehenden SAP-Landschaft und Organisation ab:​ 

  • SAP bereits umfänglich im Einsatz: Wenn SAP bereits tief in der Organisation verankert ist, existieren in der Regel klare Verantwortlichkeiten, Prozesse und ein grundlegendes Verständnis für die SAP-Anwendungen und Technologien.​ 
  • SAP als Nischenlösung: Wird SAP nur in einzelnen Bereichen z.B. in der Buchhaltung eingesetzt, sind die Erfahrungen mit SAP zu arbeiten mit etwas Geschick auch auf andere Bereiche z.B. Produktionsplanung, Logistik übertragbar. Schließlich weiß die Organisation bereits, wie SAP "tickt". 
  • SAP als Neuland: Hier muss sich erst ein breites Verständnis für die SAP-Anwendungen und Technologien entwickeln im Projektverlauf und nach dem go-live im Betrieb. In Unternehmen ohne SAP-Vorerfahrung ist zusätzlich in Change-Management, Qualifizierungsmaßnahmen und kulturelle Integration zu investieren.​ 

Je nach Ausgangssituation müssen IT-Verantwortliche ihr ITOM gezielt ausrichten – sowohl organisatorisch als auch kulturell. Der enge Schulterschluss mit den Fachbereichen ist essenziell. In allen drei Ausgangssituationen hat die S/4 HANA-Einführung eine Vielzahl von Implikationen z.B. auf die Governance,  auf die Skalierbarkeit, auf den Einsatz neuer Technologien, Arbeitsweisen und Prozesse.​ 

Kritische Erfolgsdimensionen für das ITOM​ 

Beim Design des Operating Models müssen verschiedene Schlüsselbereiche berücksichtigt werden. Viele Darstellungen beschränken sich auf die 3 Dimensionen Prozesse, Technologie und Mensch. Erfahrungsgemäß lohnt sich eine genauere Aufteilung der Schlüsselbereiche 

  • Architektur: Häufig bereits zu Projektbeginn definiert, bildet sie eine feste Rahmenbedingung – sollte aber regelmäßig auf strategische Passfähigkeit geprüft werden.​ 
  • Tooling & IT-Prozesse: Von DevOps über Monitoring bis hin zu Support-Prozessen – effiziente Toolchain und klar definierte Prozesse z.B. nach ITIL sind unerlässlich.​ 
  • Organisationsstruktur: Ob zentralisiert, dezentral oder hybrid – die Struktur muss zur operativen Realität passen.​ 
  • Prozessansatz: Funktionale oder End-to-End-Ausrichtung oder beides? Der gewählte Ansatz hat direkte Auswirkungen auf Verantwortlichkeiten und Schnittstellen.​ 
  • Way of Working: Agile, klassische oder hybride Arbeitsweisen beeinflussen das Design der Rollen und Aufgabenverteilung.​ 
  • Change Management, Ausbildung, Kompetenz: Mit Unsicherheiten und Skepsis in der Belegschaft sollte offensiv umgegangen werden. Eine fokussierte Change-Begleitung ist sehr zu empfehlen für den Gesamterfolg der Transformation. 

Nicht alle dieser Bereiche sind frei gestaltbar – viele sind durch bestehende IT-Strategien oder Unternehmensvorgaben determiniert. Daher ist es essenziell, zu Beginn des ITOM-Designs zwischen fixen Rahmenbedingungen und flexibel gestaltbaren Elementen zu unterscheiden. Mit einer S/4HANA Transformation besteht die unbedingte Möglichkeit, Strukturen zu hinterfragen und "alte Zöpfe" neu zu ordnen.​ 

Wie sich die verschiedenen Schlüsselbereiche in den Projekt- und Betriebskontext einordnen lassen und welche Aufgaben der/die ITOM-Verantwortliche währende die S/4 HANA Einführung hat, zeigt die folgende Tabelle in einer Übersicht. 

 Schlüsselbereiche für den CCOE Aufbau

Strukturierter Aufbau: Vorgehensmodell zur ITOM-Entwicklung​ 

Folgende Schritte haben sich in der Praxis bewährt:​ 

1. Benennung eines verantwortlichen ITOM Leads:  

  • Sicherstellung klarer Verantwortlichkeiten für Design und Umsetzung des Operating Models. 
  • Verankerung der Rolle auf ausreichender Hierarchieebene (z. B. direkt unterhalb der CIO-/IT-Leitung).​ 
  • Aufbau eines schlagkräftigen Kernteams mit Vertretern aus relevanten Domänen (z. B. Architektur, Betrieb, Prozesse).​ 

2. Definition von Zielbild, Prioritäten und Vision​ 

  • Erarbeitung eines klaren Zielbilds für das zukünftige ITOM: Rolle, Beitrag zur Gesamtstrategie, Abgrenzung.​ 
  • Festlegung von Leitzielen wie z. B. Skalierbarkeit, Automatisierung oder Business-Alignment.​ 
  • Identifikation und Priorisierung der größten Hebel und Handlungsfelder – z.B. auch die Etablierung neuer “Spielregeln” wie Clean Core, SAP BTP first bei Erweiterungen, durchgängige Tool Chain 

(3. Optional) Bestandsaufnahme des aktuellen TOM (As-Is)​ 

  • Erhebung vorhandener Rollen, Prozesse, Tools und Strukturen (ggf. durch Interviews, Workshops, Dokumentenanalyse).​
  • Bewertung der Reifegrade in den einzelnen ITOM-Dimensionen.​ 
  • Identifikation von Redundanzen, Lücken, Pain Points oder auch Best Practices im aktuellen Setup.​ 

4. Definition zentraler Aufgabenbereiche (Building Blocks siehe Grafik)​ 

  • Clusterung aller ITOM-Aufgabenbereiche nach strategischen, Governance- und operativen Fähigkeiten - z.B. Sourcingstrategie, Business- und IT-Zusammenarbeit, Applikations- und SAP-Basis Betrieb 
  • Orientierung an IT-Prozessen und –Funktionen sowie der Business- und IT Zusammenarbeit. 
  • Klare Abgrenzung der Aufgabenbereiche zur Vermeidung von Verantwortlichkeitslücken.​ 

Aufgabenbereiche im S/4 HANA Betrieb

5. Erste Zuordnung von Rollen zu Aufgabenbereichen (High-Level)​ 

  • Benennung von Rollen pro Aufgabenbereich – z. B. Produkt Owner, Architekt, Applikations-/Prozess-Experte.​ 
  • Berücksichtigung vorhandener Rollen in der Organisation zur Vermeidung von Doppelstrukturen.​ 
  • Differenzierung zwischen Rollen und Stellen. 

6. Detaillierung von Rollen und Verantwortlichkeiten​ 

  • Detaillierung der Aufgabenbereiche und Erstellung eines RACI-Modells zur klaren Aufgabenverteilung (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed).​ 
  • Beschreibung der Rollenprofile inklusive benötigter Kompetenzen, KPIs und Zielbilder.​ 
  • Validierung der Rollenzuordnung mit Fachbereichen, Betriebs- und Projektorganisationen. 

7. Identifikation von Lernbedarfen und Kompetenzlücken​ 

  • Ableitung von Trainingspfaden für bestehende Mitarbeitende basierend auf neuem Rollen- und Skillbedarf.​ 
  • Aufbau von Lernformaten - z. B. learning journeys, E-Learnings, SAP Learning Hub.​ 
  • Identifikation von Bedarf an Neueinstellungen oder externem Know-how - z. B. in Architektur, DevOps. 

8. Definition der Zielorganisation und Teamstrukturen​ 

  • Entwicklung eines Organisationsdesigns inkl. Teamgrößen, Berichtslinien und Verantwortlichkeiten - z.B. was gehört in das SAP CCOE und was nicht.
  • Entscheidung über Betriebsmodelle - z. B. zentral vs. dezentral, Inhouse vs. Outsourcing. 
  • Verankerung der ITOM-Strukturen im Operating Model der Gesamt-IT.
  • Organisatorischer Umgang mit neuen Themen z.B. Künstliche Intelligenz 

Ein iterativer, faktenbasierter Ansatz stellt sicher, dass das Operating Model nicht nur auf dem Papier funktioniert, sondern im operativen Alltag tragfähig ist.​ 

​ ...

Fazit​ 

Ein zielgerichtetes Operating Model für SAP S/4HANA ist ein zentraler Erfolgsfaktor für die digitale Transformation der IT-Landschaft. Es ermöglicht nicht nur einen stabilen und skalierbaren Betrieb der SAP-Plattform, sondern schafft auch die Grundlage für Innovation und Agilität. IT-Verantwortliche sollten die Gestaltung des ITOM daher als strategische Aufgabe begreifen – mit klarer Vision, strukturierter Umsetzung und konsequenter Verankerung in der Organisation. 

 ...

Torsten Scheffler ist selbständiger Berater und hat über 200 CCOE Projekte durchgeführt. 

...

Useful links: 

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-architecture-blog-posts/building-high-performing-sap-it-organization-the-art-of-organizational/ba-p/13464999

entry.title: Re: No Assignment Group in Ariba CIM for central Invoice...
entry.content:
Hi @ChristianHeck

Thank you for your question.

That message usually means the CIM rule design runtime was not provisioned correctly in the connected BTP landscape, or the app prerequisites was not completed.

This can happen after “running the booster” if:

  • Booster executed in wrong BTP subaccount
  • Prerequisite feature activation was skipped,
  • Environment provisioning did not complete successfully.

So, fix the environment binding and prerequisites first, then retry configure Assignment Group Rules.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Vendor selection in third-party process
entry.content:
Hi everyone,
I'm struggling with this problem during the third-party process.
When we make a sales order, we change the item category in ZTAS. That is the correct value for this process.
For material Z1, we can have two vendors.
The source list is maintained for vendor A and B, and A (EU vendor) is defined as fixed, while the second, B (non-EU), is not fixed. The info record is maintained also.
When we create a sales order for a specific customer , we would like the system to always select vendor B instead of A for the PR, as it is closer to the customer.
We can change the vendor manually , sure, but if the sales order has more than 200 lines, it is a very big effort.
Is there any workaround to achieve this scope without an ABAP program?

Gil

entry.title: Re: Send email notification when an existing Termination...
entry.content: This cannot be achieved using EC Business Rules or standard workflows because termination date edits do not create an effective-dated change. The supported solution is to use Intelligent Services (Job Information Changed / Termination event) with a condition on termination date change, which will reliably trigger an email notification.

entry.title: Re: #CoE#QUSS How to delete user on CBC directly?
entry.content:
Dear @davidhe79,

No you can’t delete a user in S/4 directly from CBC in a 3SL Public Cloud landscape

What you can do depends on what you mean by user:

1. Remove access to CBC

In CBC you can remove the user from the project team / deactivate or terminate their CBC access so they can’t log on to CBC / work in CBC tasks. Go to Settings ->Team

2. Delete the identity SSO user

CBC does not own the identity. If you use SSO:

  • Delete/disable the user in IAS or in your corporate IdP if that’s the source of truth
  • And let IPS provisioning update downstream assignments

SAP Notes and SAP help below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002651251

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003301639

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/user-management

3. Remove the business user in Public Cloud tenants (DEV/TEST/PROD)

If your goal is the user inside Public Cloud tenant:

  • Do it in each tenant (DEV/TEST/PROD) using the standard SAP user administration typically deactivate/lock and remove roles, deletion is often restricted/less common for audit reasons for example:
    • Maintain Business Users (F1303)

More details below:

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F1303')/S36

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/53e36b5493804bcdb3f6f14de8b487dd/e40e710321c74f28916affa9ae984bce.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Unable to print custom page range from Crystal ActiveX Report Viewer 11 in Windows 11
entry.content:
We have to support some legacy VB6 software that uses Crystal ActiveX Report Viewer 11.  Users are reporting that when printing on Windows 11 PCs and selecting a custom page range, they get a "Something went wrong" message.  Also the print preview doesn't work (but that's a lesser issue!).  We have full access to the code.  Any ideas?

PS - it works fine on Windows 10 PCs.

entry.title: Managing Performance Forms for Employees on Global Assignment
entry.content:
Dear all,

We have recently implemented a new performance management process in SuccessFactors and are currently reviewing how to manage employees on Global Assignment.

When an employee starts a Global Assignment, a new CID is created and the previous one is deactivated. We would like to understand whether it is possible to automatically launch a new performance form for the new CID at the start of the assignment, while also transferring objectives and existing information (such as comments) from the previous form to the new one. In addition, we would like the new manager to receive a notification.

Did it ever happen to you to manage this kind of topic?

Thank you very much in advance.

entry.title: What's New on SAP Analytics Cloud Home Screen 2026 Q1 QRC: New Admin Cards
entry.content:
I'm excited to introduce three new Administrator Cards on the SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Home screen in the Q1 QRC release. These cards give administrators a direct, customizable presence on users’ home screens and surface the operational insights needed to keep your SAC environment healthy and well governed with less time spent hunting for root causes.

What’s new

1 - Admin custom card

  • What it is: A fully customizable card that lets administrators display access links, announcements, and any other custom messages directly on users’ home screens.
  • How to create it: from the admin settings and under default appearance tab, click "+ Add Custom Card"

  •  What you can include: images, text, tags, and links tailored to the message or audience.

  • Important behavior: If an administrator locks and saves the custom card, users will not be able to move or hide it from their home screen.
  • Benefits: Deliver timely announcements (upgrades, maintenance windows, training links), promote key content or processes, and ensure critical guidance is seen by target audiences.

2- Administrative actions card

  • What it is: A focused card that summarizes critical recommendations for admins.
  • Purpose: Reduce time spent on root-cause analysis by surfacing key actionable items and prioritized recommendations.
  • Benefits: Enables faster, proactive housekeeping and maintenance helping admins resolve issues earlier and keep the system running smoothly.

 3 - System monitor card

  • What it is: A dedicated admin card that brings a concise, comprehensive summary of system health to the Home screen.
  • What it shows: critical system metrics, user/license health, performance hotspots, and governance-related recommendations.
  • Benefits: Empowers administrators to spot trends, identify performance issues quickly, and make informed decisions about scaling, licensing, or governance actions.

 How these cards help your organization

  • Improve visibility: Place critical notices and system health indicators where admins and users will see them daily.
  • Save time: The Administrative Actions card reduces investigation time by collecting high-priority recommendations in one place.
  • Encourage consistency: Locked custom cards ensure essential guidance (policies, access instructions, or emergency contacts) remains visible.
  • Drive governance: The System Monitor card makes it easier to track license usage, performance patterns, and governance recommendations without running separate reports.

Quick admin tips

  • Use images and tags on the custom card to increase clarity and engagement for announcements and links.
  • Lock and save a custom card only when you want fixed visibility for all users; otherwise let users personalize their Home experience.
  • Treat the Administrative Actions card as your operational to-do list, prioritize items and turn recurring recommendations into scheduled maintenance tasks.
  • Monitor the System Monitor card regularly to catch performance hotspots early and adjust configurations or scale resources as needed.

Getting started

These cards arrive with the Q1 QRC release. If you’re an admin, check your Home screen configuration options to enable and customize these cards. If you need to align messaging across teams, plan your card content and locking strategy before publishing.

entry.title: SAP IBP - Settings for TS Supply Planning
entry.content:

What are we missing to be able to attain this desired configuration?

entry.title: Re: Behind the Curtains - what's new about Maintenance Certificates
entry.content: @1jules There is no need to create a dedicated federation lead for every use case. The role of the federation lead is only important during federation setup. Once all SAP Host Agents belong to the same federation (which is a prerequisite for the Maintenance Certificate functionality), they would also be capable to gather HANA metrics of the ABAP Instances.  With other words, with the same federation lead both use cases are working fine.

entry.title: Re: Compensation Statements in People Profile
entry.content:
If you enable to People Profile block for the statements, the RBP Permissions will dictate visibility in conjunction with the visibility rules within the template.   Meaning if you give a manager permissions to the statements in RBP and enable the block in profile, they will only see the statements if the permissions are granted as default or specifically granted for that compensation template. 

I believe the other permission will dictate if you can see the statement links in the completed form or in executive review.   This may need a RBP expert to fully answer the question.

entry.title: Re: C_TADM_23 : SAP Certified S/4HANA System Administrato...
entry.content:
Hi @DieterLorenz,

The traditional C_TADM content no longer reflects how cloud operations work today — so instead of replacing it with a one‑to‑one successor, SAP is guiding learners toward several more role‑aligned paths.

The best choice depends on where you want to grow:

  • E_S4CPE SAP Certified Specialist - Implementation Consultant - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 
    Designed for consultants but very relevant for S/4HANA Cloud admins, since cloud administration shares many responsibilities with consulting in the S/4C environment.
  • C_DBADM SAP Certified Associate - Database Administrator - SAP HANA 
    Ideal if you want to focus on database administration in cloud landscapes.
  • C_ADBTP SAP Certified - SAP BTP Administrator 
    Perfect for learners moving toward SAP BTP administration, a rapidly growing cloud skill area.
Not an exam, but highly recommended
If you’re transitioning into cloud administration, make sure to complete the learning journey: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Interaction with SAP for Administrations This guided path shows you exactly how to work with the SAP Enterprise Cloud Services team — including how to submit requests and handle activities that admins previously performed themselves. Completing it earns you a Record of Achievement (ROA) and sets you up for success in cloud‑based operations.

Kind regards,
Margit

entry.title: SAP TM Report /SCMTMS/CONDITION_CC- Check and Repair consistency of conditions
entry.content:
SAP TM Report /SCMTMS/CONDITION_CC- Check and Repair consistency of conditions.

Introduction

SAP Transportation Management (TM) relies heavily on conditions to determine pricing, costs, and various business rules throughout transportation processes. However, maintaining consistency across complex transportation scenarios can be challenging, especially in large-scale implementations where data integrity issues may arise over time.

SAP Note 3533245 introduces a powerful Check & Correct Tool specifically designed to address condition-related inconsistencies in SAP TM systems. This tool, accessible via report /SCMTMS/CONDITION_CC, provides automated validation and correction capabilities for condition data, helping transportation managers maintain optimal system performance and data quality.

This blog is intended for SAP TM consultants, system administrators, and functional experts who need to ensure condition data integrity in their transportation management processes.

Understanding the Problem

Transportation management systems handle vast amounts of condition data that determine freight costs, surcharges, discounts, and other pricing elements. Common condition-related issues in SAP TM include:

Data Inconsistencies: Over time, condition records may become inconsistent due to system updates, data migrations, or manual maintenance activities. These inconsistencies can lead to incorrect pricing calculations and transportation cost determinations.

Performance Impact: Inconsistent condition data can significantly impact system performance, particularly during freight unit processing, transportation planning, and cost calculation processes. Users may experience slower response times and processing delays.

Manual Correction Challenges: Identifying and correcting condition inconsistencies manually is time-consuming and error-prone, especially in systems with thousands of condition records across multiple transportation scenarios.

SAP Note 3533245 Overview

SAP Note 3533245 addresses the critical need for automated condition validation and correction in SAP TM environments . The note provides a comprehensive solution for identifying and resolving condition-related inconsistencies that may occur during system operations.

Key Improvements: The note introduces the /SCMTMS/CONDITION_CC report, which offers systematic checking and correction capabilities for condition data integrity. This tool helps maintain optimal system performance by ensuring condition records remain consistent and accurate.

System Coverage: The correction applies to various SAP TM versions and provides essential functionality for maintaining condition data quality across different transportation management scenarios.

The Check & Correct Tool (/SCMTMS/CONDITION_CC)

What is the Tool?

The /SCMTMS/CONDITION_CC report is a specialized diagnostic and correction tool designed to validate condition data integrity within SAP TM systems . This tool integrates seamlessly with the SAP TM condition framework, providing comprehensive analysis and automated correction capabilities for condition-related inconsistencies.

The tool operates by systematically examining condition records, identifying potential issues, and offering correction options to resolve detected problems. It serves as both a diagnostic instrument and a corrective solution for maintaining optimal condition data quality.

Key Features

Comprehensive Condition Validation: The tool performs thorough validation checks across all condition types and records, identifying inconsistencies, missing data, and potential performance issues that may affect transportation processes.

Automated Correction Capabilities: Beyond identification, the tool provides automated correction functionality, allowing users to resolve detected issues efficiently without manual intervention for routine problems.

Detailed Reporting and Logging: The tool generates comprehensive reports detailing all identified issues, correction actions taken, and system status updates, providing full transparency and audit trails for condition maintenance activities.

How to Access and Use the Tool

Prerequisites

Before using the Check & Correct Tool, ensure you have the necessary system access and authorizations for condition maintenance in SAP TM. Users should possess appropriate authorization objects for transportation management and condition processing.

System Requirements: Verify that SAP Note 3533245 has been properly implemented in your system environment. The tool requires specific system components and may have dependencies on other SAP TM functionalities.

Preparation Steps: It's recommended to perform the tool execution during maintenance windows or low-activity periods to minimize impact on ongoing transportation processes. Consider creating system backups before executing correction functions.

Step-by-Step Usage Guide

  1. Navigation: Access the tool by entering transaction code /SCMTMS/CONDITION_CC in the SAP command field or navigate through the SAP TM menu structure to locate the Check & Correct Tool.
  2. Parameter Selection: Configure the tool parameters based on your specific requirements. This may include selecting condition types, date ranges, or specific transportation document types for analysis.
  3. Execution and Analysis: Execute the tool to perform condition validation checks. Review the generated analysis results to understand identified issues and their potential impact on system performance.
  4. Correction Implementation: Based on the analysis results, implement the recommended corrections using the tool's automated correction capabilities or manual intervention as appropriate.

Enter transaction SE38 or SA38 with the name of report and execute it:

Selecting the condition will show right side bellow the message:

Once execute to repair message show bellow:

Benefits and Use Cases

Business Benefits

Enhanced Data Quality: The tool significantly improves condition data quality by systematically identifying and correcting inconsistencies, leading to more accurate pricing and cost calculations in transportation processes.

Operational Efficiency: Automated validation and correction capabilities reduce manual effort required for condition maintenance, allowing transportation teams to focus on strategic activities rather than data maintenance tasks.

System Performance Optimization: By maintaining consistent condition data, the tool helps optimize system performance, reducing processing times for freight calculations and transportation planning activities.

 Conclusion

The SAP TM Check & Correct Tool for Conditions represents a significant advancement in maintaining condition data integrity within transportation management systems. By providing automated validation and correction capabilities, this tool helps organizations maintain optimal system performance while reducing manual maintenance efforts.

Implementation of SAP Note 3533245 and regular use of the /SCMTMS/CONDITION_CC report should be considered essential components of any comprehensive SAP TM maintenance strategy. The tool's ability to proactively identify and resolve condition inconsistencies makes it invaluable for maintaining system reliability and data quality.

Organizations should prioritize implementing this tool as part of their regular system maintenance routines, ensuring transportation processes continue to operate efficiently with accurate condition data supporting critical business decisions.

Refer:

3533245 - Check & Correct Tool for Conditions - SAP for Me

Hope that can help you.

Thanks, and regards

Rogerio Lima.

entry.title: Re: How to Test OAuth-Enabled Inbound Communication in SAP Using an API Client
entry.content:
No - the IAS is not involved.
Bruno is the OAuth Client and the ABAP tenant is the (combined) OAuth Authorization Server & Resource Server.

In your Communication Arrangement (which you are trying to test with Bruno) you should find "OAuth 2.0 Details" (in the "Inbound Communication" section). When you click on that a new window opens which displays the Client ID, Token Endpoint URL, Authorization Endpoint URL and a list of OAuth Scope IDs (one per exposed service).
Mark one of them an then click on "Granted by Business Catalogs".  Your user needs to be assigned to one of them.
You might have to repeat this step for the other Scopes listed on the UI.

entry.title: Skills Framework 101
entry.content:
Skills Framework 101

This blog will help you design a skills framework that is usable (not a giant spreadsheet nobody maintains).

1) What a “Skills Framework” really is

A skills framework is a structured way to define:

  • Skills (what people can do),
  • Proficiency levels (how well they can do it),
  • Roles (who needs which skills), and
  • How skills are assessed & maintained over time.

Simple definition: Skills + proficiency + role mapping + governance.

2) The 5 building blocks (I tried to keep it simple)

2.1 Skills Library

  • A list of approved skills (e.g., Stakeholder Management, SQL, Coaching, Forecasting).
  • Rule: One skill = one meaning (avoid duplicates like Stakeholder Mgmt vs Stakeholder Management).
  • Naming standard: Keep names short and clear. Add a one-line definition for every skill.

2.2 Proficiency Scale (how you measure skill strength)

Use a 4–5 level scale max. Example:

  • 1 – Basic: Understands concepts, needs guidance
  • 2 – Working: Can apply independently for standard cases
  • 3 – Advanced: Handles complex cases, improves methods, mentors others
  • 4 – Expert: Sets standards, recognized specialist

Tip: Keep definitions short and observable. Long definitions reduce adoption.

2.3 Role-to-Skills Mapping (what matters for each role)

  • For each role/job family, define top 8–15 skills (not 60).
  • For each skill, set a target proficiency (the minimum expectation).
  • Add priority: Must-have vs Good-to-have.

2.4 Assessment Method (how you capture skill levels)

Pick one starting method and expand later:

  • Self-assessment (fast, good for baseline)
  • Manager validation (improves trust)
  • Evidence-based (certifications, projects, tests) for critical skills later

2.5 Governance (who owns it & how it stays clean)

  • Owner: Skills COE / Talent / HRIT
  • Change control: Request → Review → Approve → Publish
  • Monthly hygiene: merge duplicates, retire old skills, update definitions

3) The minimum viable (MVP) skills framework

If you want a framework that actually gets adopted, start with:

  • 20–60 core skills across your first 2 job families
  • 4-level proficiency scale
  • 8–12 skills per role (with target proficiency)
  • Self-assessment + manager validation for Must-have skills

Why this works: It’s small enough to maintain and big enough to deliver value quickly.

4) Step-by-step: how to build it (practical approach)

Step 1: Decide your “skills scope”

  • Start with 1–2 job families (pilot).
  • Pick roles with clear outcomes (e.g., AE, FP&A Analyst).

Step 2: Build a clean skills list

  • Collect from: job descriptions, role profiles, top performer interviews, enablement content.
  • Normalize names (one naming standard), add short definitions.
  • Remove duplicates and overly broad skills (Communication alone is too generic).

Step 3: Define proficiency levels

  • Keep it consistent across all skills.
  • Ensure each level is observable (what you can see in actions and outputs).

Step 4: Map skills to roles

  • For each role, define:
    • Must-have skills (core)
    • Good-to-have skills (differentiators)
    • Target proficiency for each

Step 5: Decide how you will capture skills

  • Start with self-assessment to get coverage.
  • Add manager validation for Must-have skills (this improves data trust).
  • Later: connect skills to learning, gigs, internal opportunities.

Step 6: Put governance in place

  • Define who can create new skills (keep it controlled).
  • Define review cadence (monthly/quarterly).
  • Define retirement rules for unused skills.

5) Complete examples (copy/paste starters): Sales + Finance

This section gives two full examples that new organizations can adopt immediately: Job Family → Role → Skills library → Proficiency scale → Targets → Assessment → Governance.

5A) Example 1 - Job Family: Sales

5A.1 Job Family

  • Job Family: Sales
  • Purpose: Acquire and grow revenue through pipeline creation, closing, and account expansion
  • Common Roles: SDR/BDR, Account Executive (AE), Account Manager (AM), Sales Manager

5A.2 Role

  • Role Name: Account Executive (AE) - Mid Market
  • Role Summary: Owns pipeline from discovery to close; manages forecast; closes new business
  • Typical Outcomes: Qualified pipeline, closed-won deals, accurate forecast, win rate improvement

5A.3 Proficiency scale (4 levels)

LevelLabelObservable behavior
1BasicUnderstands concepts; follows playbooks with guidance
2WorkingRuns standard cycles independently; consistent results in typical cases
3AdvancedHandles complex deals; adapts approach; mentors peers
4ExpertSets standards; drives strategy; recognized authority

5A.4 Skills library (starter set for Sales)

SkillTypeShort definitionExample evidence
Discovery & QualificationFunctionalIdentifies pain, value, stakeholders, and fitClean discovery notes; clear problem + impact
Value SellingFunctionalLinks solution to measurable outcomesROI story; quantified value hypothesis
NegotiationFunctionalNegotiates terms while protecting valueTrade-offs, reduced discounting, deal desk outcomes
Pipeline ManagementFunctionalCreates and progresses opportunities through stagesStage hygiene; consistent pipeline coverage
ForecastingFunctionalProduces reliable forecast using evidence signalsForecast accuracy vs actuals
Stakeholder ManagementBehavioralBuilds influence across champions, blockers, execsMulti-threading map; exec alignment
CRM DisciplineTechnicalMaintains accurate CRM activity and stage criteriaComplete CRM records; audit-ready
Competitive PositioningFunctionalDifferentiates credibly against competitorsWin/loss notes; talk tracks

5A.5 Role → skill targets (small plus usable)

RoleSkillPriorityTarget levelWhat “good” looks like
AE – Mid MarketDiscovery & QualificationMust-have3Consistent qualification; strong close plans; crisp next steps
AE – Mid MarketValue SellingMust-have3Quantifies impact; ties to customer KPIs
AE – Mid MarketPipeline ManagementMust-have3Healthy pipeline; stage criteria met; proactive risk mgmt
AE – Mid MarketNegotiationMust-have2Protects value; avoids discount traps; uses trade-offs
AE – Mid MarketForecastingMust-have2Evidence-backed calls; fewer surprises
AE – Mid MarketStakeholder ManagementMust-have2Multi-threading; sponsor identified; blockers managed
AE – Mid MarketCRM DisciplineMust-have2Clean notes; activity maps to stage progress
AE – Mid MarketCompetitive PositioningGood-to-have2Clear differentiation; handles objections

5A.6 Assessment method

  • Baseline: Self-assessment on all mapped skills
  • Trust layer: Manager validation on Must-have skills only
  • Evidence (later): win rate, forecast accuracy, call coaching, certifications

5A.7 Governance

  • Owner: Sales Enablement + HR Talent (Skills Steward group)
  • Change control: request intake weekly; publish monthly
  • Hygiene: deduplicate quarterly; retire unused annually

5B) Example 2 - Job Family: Finance

5B.1 Job Family

  • Job Family: Finance
  • Purpose: Governance, accurate reporting, compliance, and decision support
  • Common Roles: Financial Analyst, FP&A Analyst, Accountant, Finance Business Partner (FBP), Controller

5B.2 Role

  • Role Name: FP&A Analyst
  • Role Summary: Owns budget/forecast cycles, variance analysis, and performance insights
  • Typical Outcomes: forecast accuracy, timely insights, stakeholder decisions

5B.3 Proficiency scale ( An example of 4 levels)

LevelLabelObservable behavior
1BasicUnderstands concepts; needs templates and guidance
2WorkingRuns standard analyses independently; reliable outputs
3AdvancedHandles ambiguity; builds models; influences decisions
4ExpertSets planning standards; shapes strategy; recognized authority

5B.4 Skills library (starter set for Finance)

SkillTypeShort definitionExample evidence
Budgeting & ForecastingFunctionalBuilds budgets/forecasts and runs planning cyclesCycle ownership; forecast submissions
Variance AnalysisFunctionalExplains deviations vs plan and prior periodsMonthly variance pack; drivers identified
Financial ModelingTechnicalBuilds models for scenarios and business casesSensitivity analysis; scenario model
Management ReportingFunctionalCreates exec-ready insights and KPI packsBoard/leadership pack; KPI dashboard
Cost ManagementFunctionalTracks and optimizes cost driversCost-to-serve analysis; savings tracking
Accounting BasicsFunctionalUnderstands GL, accruals, revenue/expense principlesClose support; accrual logic
Compliance & Controls AwarenessBehavioralUnderstands audit/control expectationsControl checklist; audit responses
Business PartneringBehavioralInfluences stakeholders with recommendationsDecision memos; stakeholder alignment

5B.5 Role → skill targets

RoleSkillPriorityTarget levelWhat “good” looks like
FP&A AnalystBudgeting & ForecastingMust-have3Owns cycle; improves forecast accuracy; clear assumptions
FP&A AnalystVariance AnalysisMust-have3Finds drivers fast; explains clearly to non-finance
FP&A AnalystFinancial ModelingMust-have2Models with assumptions, sensitivity, and sanity checks
FP&A AnalystManagement ReportingMust-have2Exec-ready storytelling with KPIs and actions
FP&A AnalystBusiness PartneringMust-have2Influences decisions with recommendations
FP&A AnalystAccounting BasicsGood-to-have2Understands accruals and close impacts
FP&A AnalystCompliance & Controls AwarenessGood-to-have1Understands control boundaries; escalates risks early

5B.6 Assessment method

  • Baseline: Self-assessment
  • Validation: Manager validation on Budgeting/Forecasting + Variance Analysis
  • Evidence (later): forecast accuracy trend, cycle timeliness, model quality reviews

5B.7 Governance

  • Owner: Finance COE (or Controller org) + HR Talent (Skills Steward group)
  • Change control: monthly review board for new skills + mapping changes

6) What to do in SAP SuccessFactors (so this becomes “LIVE”)

This is the execution path to operationalize Sales + Finance skills in SuccessFactors using TIH (Talent Intelligence Hub) and the People Profile.

6.1 Build the foundation (1–2 weeks)

  • Confirm your model approach: TIH as the system of record for Skills.

TIH Skills Library: centralized list of skills with status, tags, and created type.

  • Create the proficiency scale: Set up the 4-level scale (Beginner → Intermediate → Proficient → Expert).

TIH Rating Scale (Proficiency Level Scale): define proficiency levels and descriptions once, then reuse across skills and attribute types.

  • Create your Skills Library: Start with the Sales + Finance skills tables above.

TIH Skill record: maintain a clean definition, status, tags, and associated proficiency levels to keep the skills library governed and reusable.

6.2 Connect skills to roles/job profiles (1–2 weeks)

  • Job Architecture/Job Profiles: Ensure the roles exist (AE Mid Market, FP&A Analyst) in your job architecture / job profile setup.
  • Map skills to roles: Assign 8–12 skills per role and define Target Proficiency + Priority (Must-have/Good-to-have).
  • Keep it consistent: Same skill name/definition across all roles (avoid duplicates).

Role-based skill mapping (Job Profile Builder): map skills to a role and optionally maintain an expected proficiency level per skill.

6.3 Expose skills to employees and managers (1 week)

  • People Profile: Enable the Skills (TIH) section so employees can view skills.

Latest People Profile (Spotlight): Skills and Competencies are surfaced as cards for quick visibility and self-branding.

  • Permissions (RBP):
    • Employees: view/edit own skills
    • Managers: view team skills; validate/confirm where you allow
    • Skills Stewards/Admin: create/edit skills and mappings

6.4 Launch a pilot (2–4 weeks)

  • Pilot scope: Sales + Finance only (start with these two roles).
  • Campaign 1: Self-assessment on all mapped skills (simple guidance; avoid long instructions).
  • Campaign 2: Manager validation only on Must-have skills (reduces manager load and increases trust).

6.5 Make it useful fast

  • Gap reporting: Role targets vs current proficiency distribution by skill.
  • Learning linkage: Link learning items to top gap skills.
    • Sales examples: discovery, value selling, negotiation, forecasting
    • Finance examples: forecasting, modeling, variance analysis, reporting
  • Mobility readiness (optional later): identify near-ready talent based on skills targets.

6.6 Governance inside SuccessFactors - keep the data clean

  • Create a “Skills Steward” permission group: only this group can create/edit skills and role mappings.
  • Monthly library hygiene: duplicates, unused skills, naming conflicts.
  • Quarterly mapping review: refresh targets when job roles change (sales motions, finance processes, tool changes).
  • Retirement rule: retire skills unused for 12 months (or mark obsolete and map to an alternative).

7) Common mistakes (and how to avoid them)

  • Too many skills: If a role has 40 skills, nobody will complete it → keep 8–12 to start.
  • Vague skill names: Communication is too broad → specify “Executive Communication” or “Customer Communication”.
  • No governance: Without ownership, duplicates explode.
  • No use case: If skills don’t connect to hiring, learning, mobility, or performance, they die.
  • No manager involvement: Self-assessment alone often becomes optimistic data. Validate Must-haves.

Quick wins you can launch in 2–4 weeks

  • Sales + Finance pilot with 2 roles and 8–12 skills each
  • Self-assessment campaign (baseline coverage)
  • Manager validation for Must-have skills only
  • Skills gap dashboard (target vs current distribution)
  • Learning linkage for top 3 gaps per role

What's next

  • Pick your pilot population (which Sales org + which Finance org).
  • Confirm the final 8–12 skills per role and lock naming/definitions.
  • Implement the proficiency scale and skills library inSAP SuccessFactors (TIH).
  • Map skills to roles/job profiles with targets and priorities.
  • Run the pilot assessment + manager validation and publish gap insights.
  • Scale to more roles after the first 30–60 days.

Keywords: Skills Framework, Skills Taxonomy, Proficiency, Role Mapping, Governance, Talent Intelligence Hub, Skills-Based Organization, SAP SuccessFactors, Growth Portfolio

entry.title: Re: My highlights of the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release
entry.content:
Dear Sunny, 

my name is Daniel, and I am leading the Early Adopter Care Program for SAP-managed Integration. 

Thank you for your additional questions and I am happy to provide more clarity.

1. For the first phase of the Early Adopter Program we start with greenfield implementations. Meaning both systems SAP SuccessFactors EC and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition have to be provisioned from the start. Our ambition is to extend this in the next phase of the program to customers, who adopt first one Product (e.g. SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition) and after their go-live extend their landscape with additional capabilities (e.g. SAP SuccessFactors EC). 

2. Extensibility is a critical piece for sap-managed Integration. Custom field(s) are supported. 

3. Let me provide the short answer: SAP is responsible for the technical integration to ensure stable operations. CALM is used to communicate issues to the customer. Business related issues have to be resolved by the customer. My colleague @tilo_eggeling will provide additional context, as this is a comprehensive topic. 

Thanks,

Daniel
